segments.s491-g9: بیاورد is a past (narrative) third-person form meaning 'he brought/led' and here introduces the action; لشکر is its object 'an army'. گرفت is a past verb 'seized/took' referring to what the army did next, and آن حصار ('that fortress') is its object. The line uses terse, poetic coordination (no explicit 'and'), so the two clauses link as successive actions.
segments.s491-g10: بران باره is an adverbial phrase meaning 'on that occasion' or 'thereupon'; it sets the time for what follows. دژ is 'fortress/citadel' as the location, گذشتی (past 'passed') is the verb and سوار is the subject 'a rider/horseman' — the poetic word order places the place-word before the verb and subject.
segments.s491-g11: سکندر is the subject 'Alexander'. بفرمود is an honorific past verb 'he commanded/ordered'. تا introduces a subordinate clause with purpose/command 'that/so that', and جاثلیق is the person to be brought (the Catholicos). The construction means 'he ordered that the Catholicos be brought'.
segments.s491-g12: بیاورد is again the past third-person 'bring/bring here' governing the objects that follow. عراده و منجنیق are two coordinated objects 'a cart/wagon (military carriage) and a catapult/siege engine', joined by و 'and'. The verb governs both nouns.
segments.s491-g13: به یک هفته is a temporal phrase 'after one week' (literally 'to one week') setting when the action occurred. بستد is the past verb 'he besieged/closed in on' and حصار بلند is the object 'the high/lofty fortifications' (بلند is a postpositive adjective). The phrase means he completed/laid siege to the tall walls after a week.
segments.s491-g14: به شهر اندر آمد is a verb phrase 'came into the city' with به ... اندر giving 'into/within'. سپاه ارجمند is the subject 'a noble/esteemed army' (ارجمند qualifying سپاه). Poetic word order places the place and verb before the subject: 'into the city came a noble host.'
segments.s491-g15: سکندر چو آمد is a temporal clause 'when Alexander came'. به شهر اندرون means 'into the inner part of the city' (به ... اندرون = 'into the inner quarters'), so the whole segment is 'When Alexander came into the city interior'.
segments.s491-g16: بفرمود is 'he ordered/commanded' and کز introduces the subordinate content clause 'that ...'. کس is 'anyone/someone' here within the negative, and نریزند is a negative subjunctive/optative (poetic narrative form of 'not pour/ spill') giving a prohibition. خون is 'blood' used as 'shed blood' (i.e., bloodshed). Together: 'he ordered that no one shed blood.'
segments.s491-g1: چو is the classical conjunction 'when'. اسکندر is the subject (proper name). آن نامه او is a possessive phrase 'that letter of his' (نامه as direct object, او = 'his'). بخواند is a past/preterite reading verb (third-person singular) meaning 'read' (classical narrative past). The clause as a whole means 'When Alexander read that letter.'
segments.s491-g2: بزد is a past-tense verb 'he struck/blew' used with a musical instrument; نای (here a wind instrument/horn) is the object. رویین is an adjective 'iron/metal' modifying نای. و is the simple coordinating 'and'. لشکر is 'army' and براند is a verb meaning 'he drove away/put to flight'. The sequence describes an action (blew/struck the metal horn) followed by its effect (the army was driven off).
segments.s491-g3: همی رفت is an iterative/continuous past construction meaning 'kept going' or 'was going on'. یک ماه expresses duration 'for one month'. پویان functions as a participial/qualifying word meaning 'traveling/moving' (characterizing the subject). به راه is a prepositional phrase 'on/along the road'. Together: 'He kept going on the road for a month.'
segments.s491-g4: چو is again 'when'. آمد is the past 'came'. سوی is the preposition 'toward(s)'. مرز او is 'his frontier/border' (مرز modified by the possessor او). با سپاه is an instrumental/associative phrase 'with an army'. The clause: 'When he came toward his border with an army.'
segments.s491-g5: یکی پادشا بود is the common narrative opening 'there was a certain king' (یکی = 'a certain'). فریان here describes the king as possessing فر/فرّ (royal glory/splendour) — an epithet rather than a simple name. به نام is the phrase 'by the name (called)'. The clause sets up 'There was a certain king, of royal splendour, by the name ...'.
segments.s491-g6: ابا here functions as the personal name that completes 'به نام' (the provided dictionary senses are for the verb 'to refuse', so the lexical listing doesn't capture the proper-name use). لشکر و گنج و گسترده کام is a verbless coordination appended to the previous 'بود' meaning '(who) had an army and treasure and broad desires' (گسترده modifies کام = 'wide/expansive desire').
segments.s491-g7: یکی شارستان داشت is 'there was a certain town' (یکی = 'a certain') and داشت is the past 'had'. با ساز جنگ is an instrumental phrase 'with instruments/tunes of war' (ساز جنگ = war-instrument or war-music). The clause: 'There was a town that had war instruments.'
segments.s491-g8: سراپرده او is a possessive construction 'his inner chamber' (سراپرده = inner room/behind the curtain). ندیدی is a classical negative past-form meaning roughly 'one would not see' or 'you did not see' used impersonally here. پلنگ is the object 'leopard/panther'. Word order places the locative/possessive phrase before the verb: 'In his inner chamber one would not see a leopard.'
segments.s491-g73: برآشفت is a past, poetic/perfective form of آشفتن (to become upset) meaning 'he became enraged'; the subject is omitted (third person). ازان پس is an adverbial phrase 'thereupon/after that' that modifies the verb. به + دژخیم marks the indirect object/recipient ('to the torturer'), and گفت is the reporting verb in past tense introducing the following subordinate clause.
segments.s491-g74: که introduces reported speech ('that'). این هر دو را is the object phrase: این refers back to the previously mentioned pair, هر دو = 'both' (literally 'each of the two'), and را is the definite-object marker. خاک functions as the locative/instrumental notion 'in the earth' and باید (modal) before the verb expresses necessity ('must'); نهفت (from نهفتن) is the infinitive/verbal root 'to hide/bury' — together 'خاک باید نهفت' = 'must be hidden/buried in earth' (archaic word order with باید before the verb).
segments.s491-g75: چنین هم is an adverbial phrase 'thus, likewise/also' linking this order to the previous one. به + بند اندرون is a directional/locative phrase 'into/for the fetters/inner quarters' (بند = fetter/prison, اندرون = inner chamber). با + زنش ('با زنش') indicates accompaniment 'together with his wife' (با = 'with', زن = 'wife/woman', ش = possessive 'his'). The object(s) to be bound are understood from context.
segments.s491-g76: به شمشیر هندی is an instrumental/adverbial phrase 'with an Indian sword' (به used here as 'by/with' + هندی 'Indian'). بزن is an imperative form 'strike!' addressed to the agent; گردنش is the object 'his neck' with the enclitic possessive ش attached. Word order places the instrumental phrase before the imperative and the possessed noun after the verb.
segments.s491-g77: سکندر (Alexander) is the subject. بیامد is the past tense 'came'. زمین بوس داد is a compact expression meaning 'gave a kiss to the ground' — بوس here is a noun 'a kiss' and داد 'gave' forms the verbal phrase 'kissed the ground'. The clause sequence lists his actions in past narrative order.
segments.s491-g78: بدو ('to him') is the indirect-object clitic phrase marking the addressee of گفت. گفت is the past reporting verb 'said' introducing the vocative that follows. کای is the archaic/vocative interjection 'O', and شاه قیصر نژاد is the vocative address 'king, of Caesar's stock/lineage' (نژاد = lineage). The order is typical for direct address after a reporting verb.
segments.s491-g79: اگر introduces a conditional clause 'if'. خون ایشان is the object 'their blood' (خون = blood/bloodshed, ایشان = they/them). ببخشی is a second-person singular present/subjunctive (or present-future conditional) of بخشیدن used here in the sense 'you forgive/spare' (classical phrasing: 'if you pardon'). به من marks the beneficiary 'to/for me'.
segments.s491-g80: سرافراز گردم is a predicative clause meaning 'I shall become honored/proud' — گردم is the present/subjunctive form of گشتن used here to express result or potential ('I will become'). به هر انجمن is an adverbial phrase 'before/in every assembly' ('هر' = every, انجمن = assembly), specifying the sphere in which the speaker will be honored.
segments.s491-g97: چنین گفت = “thus said.” چنين (adverb) precedes the verb to give a literary/poetic ‘thus’ or ‘so’. گفت is the past-tense verb ‘said’ with an omitted subject (pro-drop). با + name here introduces the addressee (archaic/poetic use: ‘said to X’). بیقطون قیدروش is a proper name or epithet (together the addressee), placed after با.
segments.s491-g98: که introduces a reported clause ‘that…’. ز + او (written here as زو) = ‘from her/from him’ (poetic dz- form) and is the source-mark; in the manuscript words it is split (ز + و) but together read as ‘زو’ ‘from her’. بر + دارم (here in the negative بر ندارم) is the verbal compound برداشتن ‘to take up/away’; بر is the separable verbal prefix and ندارم is the first‑person negative present, so بر ندارم = ‘I will not take away’ (or ‘I do not take away’). دل و چشم و گوش are the direct objects (‘heart, eye, and ear’) of برداشتن. The whole clause therefore states the speaker will not take his heart/eyes/ears away from her.
segments.s491-g99: چگونه = ‘how’ (question/ rhetorical). مر is an older dative/clitic meaning ‘to me’ or ‘for me’ (used idiomatically to mark possession/relation to the speaker); او را = ‘her/him’ with the object marker را. ندارم is first‑person present negative ‘I do not have/possess’. Together 'چگونه مر او را ندارم' is a rhetorical ‘how could I not have her (for myself)?’. چو before جان here means ‘since/for/as’ (giving the reason), and جان in this phrase functions as ‘life’ or ‘beloved’ (i.e. ‘she is my life’), linking into the explanatory next segment.
segments.s491-g100: کزو = از + او ‘from/of whom’ (relative/ablative: ‘from her/him’). یافتم is 1st‑person past ‘I found/obtained’. جفت here means ‘mate/pair’ (i.e. a fitting partner), and شیرین‌روان is a compound adjective ‘sweet‑souled’ (sweet in spirit). Grammatically, the clause says that from her the speaker found a match and a sweet‑souled companion (supporting the rhetorical question in the previous segment).
segments.s491-g41: شد is the past, perfective verb 'became/was' indicating a change of state. آن مرد is the definite subject 'that man'. دستور here is used as a nominal condition/arrangement (a poetic use: 'his state/condition'); با + درد forms a prepositional phrase 'with pain/distress', and جفت follows to mean 'paired/joined' (i.e. his condition was paired with pain). Word order is Verb – Subject – Predicate (classical/poetic inversion: 'شد' + subject + predicate).
segments.s491-g42: ندانست is the past negative of دانستن 'he did not know'. کان را functions as 'that (thing) / therein' (classical demonstrative/relativizer marked by the object marker را). چه باشد نهفت is an indirect question: چه 'what' + باشد (present/subjunctive copula here, 'may be / is') + نهفت 'hidden/veiled' — literally 'what may be hidden'. The clause is the object of ندانست.
segments.s491-g43: ازان پس is a temporal connector 'after that / thereupon'. بدو is the dative clitic form 'to him'. گفت is the past verb 'said'. شاه جهان is a title phrase 'king (شاه) of the world/realm (جهان)' functioning as the speaker; Persian places the speaker noun after گفت in this poetic order.
segments.s491-g44: The first که introduces reported speech 'that'. این کار 'this deed/affair' is the topic. باید is a modal 'must / it is necessary'; the second که introduces the subordinate clause governed by the necessity, and ماند (present/subjunctive) means 'remain' (i.e. 'it must remain'). نهان is the predicate adjective 'hidden'. Together: 'that this deed must remain hidden.'
segments.s491-g45: مرا is the first‑person object 'me' (here the person to be brought/placed). چون فرستادگان is a comparative phrase 'like/as the envoys'. پیش خوان is a locative phrase 'before the banquet/at the table' (پیش 'in front of / before', خوان 'feast/table/banquet' — Persian often strings such nouns to indicate place). The overall meaning is an instruction to present the speaker before the feast as envoys are presented.
segments.s491-g46: سخنهای is the plural noun 'words / speeches / verses' and is the object of the following verb. قیدافه is an obscure proper name (an item/collection being named). چندی here functions adverbially 'for a while / a little (bit) / a few [lines]'. بران in this context is an imperative-like verbal form meaning 'bring forth / recite / set forth' (poetic usage). So: 'Recite/bring forth for a while the words of Qeydafeh.'
segments.s491-g47: مرا is the object 'me' for the imperative verb بفرست 'send!'. شاد here is adverbial 'cheerfully / in gladness' modifying the imperative. با ده سوار is an accompaniment phrase 'with ten horsemen' (با = 'with', ده 'ten', سوار 'horsemen/riders'). The imperative bferest governs the whole request.
segments.s491-g48: که introduces a purpose/result clause 'so that / and'. رو نامه‌بر here names the agent 'the letter‑carrier / courier' (رو is part of the verbal/compound phrase meaning 'let go/send the courier' in this poetic phrasing). زود is an adverb 'quickly/at once', and و پاسـخ بیار contains the conjunction و 'and' plus the imperative بیار 'bring' with پاسـخ 'answer' as its object — 'and bring an answer'. The clause expresses the additional order to send a fast courier and return with a reply.
segments.s491-g49: بدو is the prepositional phrase 'to him/unto him' (clitic-like preposition + object), followed by the name بیطقون (Biṭqūn) as the indirect object; گفت is the past-tense verb 'said'; کایدون is the patient/name 'Kāydūn' (what to do with him); کنم is the 1st-person present/subjunctive 'I do / shall do' used here to report the speaker's question/plan ('what shall I do (to Kāydūn)?'). Word order is canonical for Persian reporting: indirect object + گفت + complement clause with the subjunctive verb at the end.
segments.s491-g50: به فرمان برین is an adverbial phrase 'by/under this command' — به 'by' (instrumental/agentive), فرمان 'command', and برین = بر + این (literary/poetic 'on/over this' / 'this'); چاره افسون کنم is the predicate 'I will (attempt to) enchant a remedy' with چاره 'a remedy' and افسون 'a charm/spell', and کنم again as 1st-person subjunctive/future-like verb at the end. The verb-final positioning and omission of an overt copula are typical of poetic Persian.
segments.s491-g51: به شبگیر is a temporal/qualifying phrase 'at nightfall' or 'with nocturnal onset' (به + شبگیر), خورشید is the subject 'the sun' (postpositive adjective شبگیر precedes it), and خنجر کشید is a verbal phrase 'drew (a) dagger' (کشید past of کشیدن). The verb follows the object (خنجر) and is in simple past; the image is idiomatic/poetic (the sun 'drawing a dagger' = setting).
segments.s491-g52: شب تیره is a nominal phrase 'the dark night'; از بیم is an adverbial 'from/because of fear' (از + بیم); شد ناپدید uses شد (become/happened) as a light verb/copula 'became' plus the predicate adjective ناپدید 'vanished/invisible' — together meaning 'the dark night, out of fear, became vanished' (i.e. fled).
segments.s491-g53: نشست is past 'sat/settled' (here best read as 'rose from' or 'moved off and then sat'); از بر تخت is an ablative phrase 'from off the throne' (از + بر + تخت, an archaic/poetic double preposition meaning 'from upon the throne'); بر بیطقون is a locative 'before/at Biṭqūn' (بر used as 'at/upon' with the proper name). The sequence omits a coordinating verb between motions, a common compactness in epic narration.
segments.s491-g54: پر از شرم رخ دل پر از آب خون is a pair of nominal predications using the pattern 'پر از X' = 'full of X'. First: رخ پر از شرم — 'his face full of shame'; second: دل پر از آبِ خون — 'his heart full of water and blood' (literally 'water [and] blood'); the copula is omitted, so these are descriptive verbless clauses stacked for poetic effect.
segments.s491-g55: سکندر is the subject 'Sikandar/Alexander'; به پیش اندرون is a directional/locative phrase 'forward into the inner room' or 'toward the inner quarters' (به + پیش 'forward/in front' + اندرون 'inside/inner chamber'); با کمر begins an instrumental/attendant phrase 'with (a) girdle/waist...' where کمر ('girdle/belt' here) will be qualified by the next segment. The verb of motion is implied across the phrase (typical condensed epic style).
segments.s491-g56: گشاده modifies the preceding کمر: 'with the girdle loosened' (گشاده = open/loose). درچاره is an adverbial qualifier 'in distress / seeking remedy'; و is the simple conjunction 'and'; بسته در is a noun phrase 'the door closed' (بسته 'closed' modifying در 'door' — note در is 'door' here). The clause strings these descriptive states together without an overt main verb — a compact descriptive sequence.
segments.s491-g81: A fronted noun phrase + interrogative + verb phrase. سر بیگناهان (literally 'the heads of the innocent') is the object/topic placed at the start for emphasis. چه is the interrogative 'what/what gain', and بری here is an archaic/poetic verbal form meaning 'to cut off / to take' (compare classical بریدن) and functions as the core action. به کین is a purposive/causal prepositional phrase 'for/out of vengeance'.
segments.s491-g82: Subordinate clause introduced by که 'that/which'. نپسندد is a present/subjunctive-style negative verb meaning 'would not approve / does not approve' (used here to express judgment). از ما جهان‌آفرین literally 'by/from our World‑Creator' names God as the judge whose approval is lacking.
segments.s491-g83: Word order here is inverted: بدو 'to him' precedes گفت 'said', followed by the speaker's name/epithet بیداردل بیطقون. The clause therefore reads 'Bīdardal Bīṭaqūn said to him.' گفت is the past tense verb 'said'.
segments.s491-g84: Reported content introduced by که 'that'. آزاد کردی is a compound: آزاد (free) + کردی (you did) = 'you freed' (past, 2nd person). دو تن را is the definite/accusative object 'two persons' (را marks the object). ز خون is an ablative phrase 'from blood' (i.e. from bloodshed or death).
segments.s491-g85: سبک in front modifies the manner ('lightly' / 'in a slight tone'); followed by the speaker's name بیطقون and گفت 'said'. با + قیدروش indicates the manner or accompaniment — 'with quyd‑ruš' (here quyd‑ruš is used as a noun meaning 'restraint, manner' or a technical phrase for a binding/constraint), so با قیدروش = 'with a constrained/mannered tone' or 'with that fettering phrase'.
segments.s491-g86: که again introduces reported content: بردی is past 2nd-person 'you took/carried' (here 'you cut off / you took away'). سر is 'head' and is modified by the participial phrase دور مانده ز دوش 'having remained apart/from the shoulder' — دور مانده = 'left distant/removed', ز دوش = 'from the shoulder'. The whole means 'you took the head that had been left off the shoulder.'
segments.s491-g87: فرستم کنون is present/future 'I will send / I send now' (contemporary or imminent action). با تو 'with you' marks accompaniment. او را is the definite object 'him' (را marks object). بهم here means 'together / along (with you)', indicating he will go in your company.
segments.s491-g88: بخواند is a future/optative sense 'he/she will have (him) called / will summon' (here indicates someone will cause him to be summoned). به مادرت (مادَر + ت) = 'to/at your mother' ('-ت' is the 2nd‑person possessive suffix). بر بیش و کم is an idiomatic phrase 'concerning greater and lesser [matters]' — i.e. to answer for big and small faults/affairs. The clause as a whole: '(She) will have him summoned before your mother to answer for greater and lesser.'
segments.s491-g89: اگر introduces a conditional clause ('if'). ساو و باژم are coordinated items (here personal names) serving as the object of فرستد. فرستد is the 3rd-person singular present/formal narrative of فرستادن 'to send' and governs the coordinated direct object; نکوست is a predicate meaning 'is good' attached to the whole conditional clause.
segments.s491-g90: کسی را is the object/topic phrase ('anyone' with the definite/object marker را). ندرد is the negative of درَدن/دَرَیدن (n‑ + present-stem) meaning 'does not / will not tear' here describing what will not happen. بدین is a prepositional phrase 'by/with this' (به + این in older/poetic word-division), followed by جنگ 'battle/war' and پوست 'skin' — together expressing the idea 'in/by this battle no one’s skin will be torn.'
segments.s491-g91: نگه کن is an imperative construction meaning 'pay attention/heed' (نگه as noun/verb-stem + کن imperative of کردن). بدین پاک دستور من is a prepositional noun phrase: بدین (به + این) 'to/at this', پاک 'pure/clear', دستور 'order/command', من 'of mine' — together: 'to this clear order of mine.'
segments.s491-g92: که introduces a subordinate clause ('which/that'). گوید is the verbal predicate here ('says' / 'will tell') in the narrative present; بدو is the dative pronoun 'to him'. رزم‌گر سور من is a genitive noun phrase where رزم‌گر 'warrior/war‑man' is modified by سور (here a technical/poetic word — e.g. 'trumpet/war‑signal' or a role) and followed by من as possessive 'my' — literally 'my rasmgar(sur)'.
segments.s491-g93: تو آن کن is an imperative instruction: 'You, do that' (تو subject emphatic + آن 'that' + کن imperative 'do'). ز خوبی که او با تو کرد is a causal relative clause: ز 'from/because of' + خوبی 'kindness' modified by the relative clause introduced by که — 'that he did to you' (او subject, با تو 'to you', کرد 'did').
segments.s491-g94: به پاداش is an adverbial 'as a reward / in return'. پیچد is the 3rd‑person singular present/subjunctive of پیچیدن 'to turn/twist' and here functions optatively/forecasting ('may/ will turn' or 'turns'). دل رادمرد is the patient phrase 'the nobleman's heart' (دل 'heart' + رادمرد 'noble/man of courage').
segments.s491-g95: چو is a temporal/conditional conjunction meaning 'when/if'. این پاسخ‌نامه 'this reply/response-letter' is the object. یابی is the 2nd‑person present/subjunctive/optative form of یافتن 'to get, find, receive' ('you get/you should get/when you receive'). ز شاه is the source phrase 'from the king.'
segments.s491-g96: به خوبی is an adverbial phrase 'with kindness / kindly'. ورا is the object pronoun 'him/it' (و + را = 'to him' / 'him' as direct object in older style). بازگردان is the imperative 2nd‑person singular of بازگرداندن 'return/send back'. ز راه 'on the way / by the road' indicates direction or sending off on his way.
segments.s491-g33: تو (you) is fronted for address/emphasis and بنشین is an imperative (2nd-person singular) meaning “sit down” or “take a seat.” The prepositional phrase به آیین و رسم کیان follows and qualifies how the addressee should sit: به + noun functions adverbially as “in/according to,” آیین و رسم are near-synonyms “rites/customs,” and کیان is the genitive/attributive noun “the Kians/the royal house.”
segments.s491-g34: چو introduces a temporal clause “when.” من آیم is a first-person present/subjunctive form of آمدن used here as a simple temporal “when I come.” پیشت is a locative/enclitic meaning “before you / in your presence.” The verbless phrase کمر بر میان is elliptical: کمر (waist/girdle) + بر (on/upon) + میان (middle) — i.e. ‘gird/fasten the belt about the middle.’ The main clause’s imperative sense carries over into this elliptic instruction.
segments.s491-g35: بفرمای here is a reporting/command verb (classical/polite past or narrative use of فرمود) meaning roughly “he said/ordered.” تا introduces the subordinate/result clause “that/so that.” گردن is the direct object “neck,” and قیدروش is a compound noun here meaning a neck-fetter/chain (an object to be removed); together گردن قیدروش = “the chain at the neck.”
segments.s491-g36: ببرد is a third-person form of بردن used here in the reported/subjunctive sense “(should) take/remove.” The agent phrase دژآگاه جنگی strings two nouns/adjectives: دژآگاه (fortress-warden) qualified by جنگی (warlike/military), i.e. ‘the warlike fortress-warden.’ ز is the poetic genitive/preposition “from,” and دوش means “shoulder” (not ‘last night’ here) indicating from where the chain is removed.
segments.s491-g37: من آیم is first-person “I come” (present/subjunctive used for simple future/intent). به پیشت = “to your presence / before you,” where the first به is directional “to.” به خواهشگری is an adverbial phrase “in entreaty / as a petitioner” (the second به makes خواهشگری function adverbially), so the whole clause says the speaker will come before the addressee in the posture of petitioning.
segments.s491-g38: نمایم is first-person singular (I) of نمودن/نمایدن “to show” (here ‘I will show’ or ‘I will make manifest’). فراوان is adverbial “greatly / abundantly.” The clitic ت (here written separate) plus را marks the addressee as object (ت = “you” enclitic, را = object marker), and کهتری (inferiority/humility) is the noun shown—so the clause means “I will show you great humility / make myself greatly inferior before you.”
segments.s491-g39: نشستنگهی is an archaic/poetic noun from نشستنگه ‘seat/place to sit’ and here names the seat or sitting place. ساز (literally “device/instrument”) functions as ‘arrangement’ or ‘made/prepared’ when attached to nouns in older Persian, so نشستنگهی ساز = “the seat thus prepared.” بی‌انجمن is an adjectival phrase “without assembly/alone / apart from the council,” modifying the seat as solitary or set apart.
segments.s491-g40: چو here carries a conditional/temporal sense “if/when.” خواهش is “request/entreaty,” and فزایم is first-person present/subjunctive of فزودن “to increase,” meaning “if my entreaty increases.” ببخشی is second-person present/subjunctive/optative of بخشیدن used here as “you grant/give” (i.e. ‘may you grant’ or ‘you will grant’), and بمن is the indirect object “to me.”
segments.s491-g25: This clause is a main clause with subject (سکندر) + past verb (بدانست). کان introduces the embedded identification clause that follows; here it functions like an archaic/demonstrative 'that' pointing to the man. کی + ست is the copular question-word phrase 'who is' (کی 'who' + ست 'is') forming the content of what Alexander realized.
segments.s491-g26: بجستش is a past/transitive verb with the clitic -ش 'him' (he asked/searched him). The subordinate clause is introduced by که as a complementizer 'what/that' and contains درمان 'remedy' + آن کار 'that affair' as its object. چی + ست at the end is the interrogative copula 'what is' (چی 'what' + ست 'is'), so the whole clause asks 'what is the remedy for that matter?'.
segments.s491-g27: بفرمود is an honorific/polite past verb 'he commanded/said'. تا here functions as a subordinating marker 'so that / in order that' introducing the result clause. پیش او شد وزیر literally orders the sequence 'before him became the vizier' — شد is the past/become copula used to form 'was made/was brought' and وزیر is the noun 'vizier'. Word order mirrors classical Persian: verb (بفرمود) then subordinate purpose/result clause.
segments.s491-g28: بدو is the dative/adverbial prepositional form 'to him'. داد is the past verb 'gave' (not 'justice' here). The list فرمان و تاج و سریر are the direct objects (a command/decree, a crown, and a throne) presented after the verb in typical Persian coordination with و between items.
segments.s491-g29: This is a classical/poetic word order: خردمند را (the wise man, topicalized by را) + بیطقون (the proper name) + بود نام (lit. 'was name') — the inversion 'X را Y بود نام' equals 'X was named Y.' Here را marks the topical/definite noun rather than a direct object.
segments.s491-g30: یکی رای begins a nominal phrase 'one counsel/one plan.' The sequence زن مرد is a terse juxtaposition: زن followed by مرد, with مرد modified by the postpositive adjective phrase گسترده کام ('gostaṛdeh-kām' literally 'wide-desired' or 'of broad desire'). Grammatically گسترده کام functions as an adjectival/epithet describing مرد; the whole segment says there was 'one counsel' concerning a man of broad desires (and/or a plan about a woman).
segments.s491-g31: بدو گفت is the common construction 'he said to him' (بدو = to him; گفت = said). کاید here is a noun 'the plan/plot' (an announcement of the intended course of action). به پیشت عروس places the object 'the bride' before the addressee: به پیشت 'before you' (پیشت = in your presence) + عروس 'bride'.
segments.s491-g32: ترا is written in two parts here (ت + را) but functions as the object pronoun 'your'/'you' (ت = 2nd-person suffix, را = object marker). خوانم is a 1st-person present/future-like verb 'I will call/summon' used to report the announced action. اسکندر فیلقوس is the vocative or appositive name 'Alexander Philoquus' attached at the end — the person being addressed or named in the speech.
segments.s491-g57: چون begins a temporal/adverbial clause ('when' / 'as'); آن پور قیدافه is a demonstrative + nominal phrase 'that son of Qeydafeh'; را is the definite/object marker attached to that noun phrase; شهرگیر follows as an appositive/title ('the city's conqueror') qualifying the same person. The main verb for this clause appears in the next segment.
segments.s491-g58: بیاورد is a past (3rd-person) narrative verb here ('brought' / 'was brought' in the temporal clause started by چون). گریان is a participial/adjectival word 'weeping' describing the person's state, گرفته is a past participle 'taken/seized', and اسیر is a predicative noun 'a captive' — together they describe how he was when brought.
segments.s491-g59: زنش is زن + enclitic possessive ش ('his wife' or 'his woman'); هم چنان نیز is an adverbial cluster meaning 'also / likewise / just so'; با introduces an instrumental phrase 'with' and بوی و رنگ ('scent and colour') here means 'in perfume and appearance' — a conventional poetic way to describe a woman's beauty and adornment.
segments.s491-g60: گرفته is a perfect participle 'having seized / taken'; جوان is the object 'the young (man)' here; چنگ او را is a definite object phrase 'his hand/claw' (چنگ used for a grasp/hand), with را marking the object; به چنگ is a prepositional/idiomatic phrase 'by the grip / with a grasp' describing the manner in which she held him.
segments.s491-g61: سبک بیطقون is a personal name/epithet (here read as 'Sabk-e Bitqun') functioning as the speaker; گفت (here) is the reporting verb 'said'; کین (classical demonstrative written کین) = 'this' introduces the demonstrative phrase; مرد 'man' is the noun; کی+ست is the inverted interrogative 'who is' (کی 'who', ست = copula 'is').
segments.s491-g62: This clause expresses an exclamatory/question about suffering. کش is an archaic/exclamatory particle here (not well represented by the listed senses) that frames the exclamation; از 'from' marks source/reason; درد here is emotional/physical 'pain' or 'sorrow'; چندین 'many / how many'; بباید is a modal 'one must / it is necessary'; گریست is the infinitive/presentive form 'to weep' or 'one must weep' — overall: 'From such pain, how many times must one weep?'
segments.s491-g63: چنین داد پاسخ is a fixed narrative formula 'thus gave answer' = 'he replied thus'; که introduces the reported content that follows; بازآر is an imperative/causative form 'bring back / restore' (here 'regain'); هوش 'sense / wits' — together 'restore your wits' or 'bring back your senses.'
segments.s491-g64: که (conjunction) again introduces the identification clause; من 'I' is the subject; پور is 'son' (here part of the name-form or patronymic 'Pour-…'); قیدافه‌ام is the proper name with the copular/possessive suffix -ام ('I am Qeydafeh' or 'I am Pour-Qeydafeh'); قیدروش follows as an epithet/name (here treated as part of the speaker's name). Grammatically this is a self-identification: 'I am Pour Qeydafeh Qeydrosh.'
segments.s491-g17: یکی (indefinite) introduces an indefinite subject: “a certain / one.” پور قیدافه is a patronymic/nominal phrase (پور = 'son of' + Qeydafeh as the name). داماد بود is the predicate: داماد (son‑in‑law) + past copula بود (‘was’). Word order is typical Persian: subject phrase then nominal predicate with بود in past tense.
segments.s491-g18: بدین شهر is a prepositional/locative phrase meaning “in this/that city” (بدین = in this/that, here written as prefix+demonstrative). فریان is the subject (poetic plural/collective of فر, ‘farr’ = royal glory) and بدو is the dative pronoun ‘to him.’ شاد بود is the predicate: شاد (glad) + بود (was). The line uses inverted poetic order but follows the pattern Locative – Subject – Dative – Predicate.
segments.s491-g19: بدو داده بدِ دختر ارجمند should be read as دادَه بود construction: داده (past participle ‘given’) + (بود) auxiliary = ‘had given.’ بدو is the dative ‘to him’ (recipient) and دختر ارجمند is the direct object ‘a noble/esteemed daughter.’ In classical/poetic Persian the auxiliary may be compressed in writing.
segments.s491-g20: کلاهش is literal ‘his hat’ used figuratively for status (“his cap / standing”); به قیدافه is a locative/agentive phrase ‘at/in Qeydafeh’ (به + place), and گشته is perfect participle / resultative ‘has become’ (or ‘was made’), followed by بلند ‘high/lofty.’ Together they mean his status/hat had been made lofty at Qeydafeh. The verb is participial, typical of narrative poetry.
segments.s491-g21: که introduces a subordinate/relative clause here (‘that’/‘who’). داماد را نام بد قیدروش is a standard Persian ditransitive pattern: X را نام Y دادن = 'to give X the name Y.' را marks داماد as the patient/recipient of the naming; نام (name) is modified by بد (bad) and followed by the epithet/name قیدروش. The whole phrase means ‘they gave the son‑in‑law the bad name “Qeyd‑rosh”.’
segments.s491-g22: بدو داده فریان دل و چشم و گوش: word order is inverted for poetic effect. فریان (the farr, subject) + داده (given) + بدو (to him) = ‘the farr had given him…’ followed by the list دل و چشم و گوش (heart, eye, and ear) as the things granted. The participial/compound verbal form expresses a completed bestowal.
segments.s491-g23: یکی مرد بد نام او شهرگیر: یکی introduces an indefinite ‘a certain’; مرد بد‌نام is a noun + postposed adjective meaning ‘an ill‑famed man’ (بدنام = ill‑reputed). او here is the pronoun in apposition/emphasis and شهرگیر is an appositive epithet ‘town‑besieger’ (one who seizes towns). The phrase strings appositions/epithets in the characteristic epic/poetic order.
segments.s491-g24: به دستش = prepositional phrase ‘by his hand’ with the possessive suffix ش attached to دست. زن و شوی (here intended as زن و شوهر ‘woman and husband’) is a coordinated object. گشته اسیر is a participial/resultative construction: گشته (become/been made) + اسیر (captive) = ‘were made captive / had become captives.’ The sequence forms a passive/result clause (‘by his hand a woman and husband were captured’).
segments.s491-g65: جزین is the fused poetic form of جز این (“except this”) placed before the noun it limits. دخت فریان is a noun phrase ‘the (radiant/glorious) maiden’; مرا نیست جفت is an older Persian existential/construction meaning ‘for me there is no pair/match’ (مرا = ‘for me’, را is the object marker attached after the clitic here, and نیست = ‘is not’). The overall order is exception phrase + noun, then the existential about the speaker.
segments.s491-g66: که introduces a relative clause ‘who/that’. دارد here functions as a dynamic verb ‘has/keeps’ (classical use meaning ‘holds’ or ‘keeps’). پس پرده is a locative phrase ‘behind a curtain/veil’ (پس = ‘behind/after’, پرده = ‘veil’). من نهفت is a compact way of saying ‘hidden from me’ (من marks the person affected; نهفت = ‘hidden/concealed’).
segments.s491-g67: برآنم is a fixed phrase ‘I am resolved/intend’. که introduces the subordinate clause. او را is the direct object marker sequence (او = ‘her/ him’, را = definite object marker). سوی خان خویش is a directional phrase ‘toward my own khan/house/seat’ (سوی = ‘toward’, خان = title/house, خویش is the reflexive/possessive ‘own’ formed by خوی + ش). Word order: main predication (برآنم) + that-clause giving the intended action.
segments.s491-g68: برم is the present/future subjunctive-like form of بردن ‘to take’ (here ‘I will take/carry’). تا introduces a purposive clause ‘so that / in order that’. بدارمش is an older/ambiguous verbal form with b- prefix + دار + object suffix ش; in many contexts it can mean ‘to keep/hold’ but can also mean ‘to hang’ — here it expresses the speaker’s intended action toward the woman and takes the enclitic object -ش (‘her’). چون جان خویش is a simile ‘as/like my own life’ (چون = ‘like’, جان = ‘life/soul’, خویش = ‘own’).
segments.s491-g69: اسیرم is the predicative adjective+nominative clitic meaning ‘I am captive’ (اسیر + -م ‘I’). کنون is the adverb ‘now’. در کف is an idiom meaning ‘in the hand of / under the control of’ (در = ‘in’, کف = ‘palm/hand’). شهرگیر is the agent/possessor in that idiom ‘the besieger’ (شهرگیر = ‘conqueror/attacker’). Word order follows classical Persian: state + temporal adverb + prepositional idiom + possessor.
segments.s491-g70: روان خسته is a nominal phrase ‘(my) spirit/soul weary’ (روان = ‘soul/spirit’, خسته = ‘tired/weary’). از اختر is a prepositional phrase ‘because of/from the star/fate’ (از = ‘from/because of’, اختر = ‘star’ often used figuratively for fate/fortune). و links the coordination to تن به تیر ‘and (my) body with arrows’ (تن = ‘body’, به تیر = ‘by/with arrows’). The whole clause describes physical and spiritual exhaustion and wounding.
segments.s491-g71: چو is the temporal conjunction ‘when’. بشنید is the simple past ‘he heard’. ز (here contracted with the following element) means ‘from’ (poetic/archaic form of از). این سخن is the object ‘this speech’, and بیطقون is the personal name ‘Bitqun’ that is the experiencer; word order is slightly inverted compared with modern Persian but common in epic poetry.
segments.s491-g72: سرش گشت is a verbal clause ‘his head became/turned’ (سر = ‘head’, ش = possessive ‘his’, گشت = ‘became/turned’). پر درد و دل پر ز خون uses پر = ‘full (of)’ twice in parallel: ‘full of pain’ for the head (درد = physical pain) and ‘heart full of blood’ (دل = heart, ز = ‘with/of’, خون = blood). This is a compact poetic description of grievous wounding and inner anguish.

sentences.s491-s5: He brought an army and the army seized that fortress; on that occasion a mounted rider passed the citadel.
sentences.s491-s6: Alexander ordered that the Catholicos be brought a cart and a catapult.
sentences.s491-s7: After a week he invested the high ramparts; a noble army entered into the city.
sentences.s491-s8: When Alexander entered the inner city, he commanded that no one spill blood.
sentences.s491-s1: When Alexander read that letter, he struck the iron (metal) reed/trumpet and put the army to flight.
sentences.s491-s2: He kept going for a month along the road; when he came toward his frontier with an army, ...
sentences.s491-s3: There was a certain king, possessed of royal splendour, called Aba — (he) had an army and treasure and wide desires.
sentences.s491-s4: There was a town with war-instruments; in his inner chamber one would not see a leopard.
sentences.s491-s37: He grew angry thereupon and said to the torturer, "These two must be buried in the earth."
sentences.s491-s38: Do likewise: bind them inside the inner chamber with his wife, and with an Indian sword strike his neck.
sentences.s491-s39: Alexander came, kissed the ground, and (to him) said, 'O king, of Caesar's lineage.'
sentences.s491-s40: If you pardon their blood, I shall be honored in every assembly.
sentences.s491-s49: Thus he said to Biqatun Qeydrowsh, “I will not take my heart, eyes, and ears away from her.”
sentences.s491-s50: How could I not have her, when she is my life — from whom I found a mate and one sweet of soul?
sentences.s491-s21: That man was stricken with painful distress; he did not know what was hidden there.
sentences.s491-s22: After that the king of the realm said to him that this deed must remain hidden.
sentences.s491-s23: Bring me forward, like the envoys, before the banquet; recite for a while the (verses/sayings of) Qeydafeh.
sentences.s491-s24: Send me off in joy with ten horsemen; dispatch a swift courier at once and bring back an answer.
sentences.s491-s25: He said to Biṭqūn, "What shall I do with Kāydūn? By this command I will use a charm as a remedy."
sentences.s491-s26: At nightfall the sun drew a dagger; the dark night, from fear, became vanished.
sentences.s491-s27: He rose from the throne and sat before Biṭqūn; his face full of shame, his heart full of water and blood.
sentences.s491-s28: Sikandar (Alexander) went forward into the inner chamber with his girdle loosened — in distress — and the door closed.
sentences.s491-s41: What gain is there in cutting off the heads of the innocent for vengeance — something the Creator of the world would not approve?
sentences.s491-s42: To him Bīdardal Bīṭaqūn said: 'You have freed two people from blood.'
sentences.s491-s43: Bīṭaqūn (lightly) said with restraint/manner: 'You took a head that had been left off the shoulder.'
sentences.s491-s44: I will now send him with you; she will have him summoned before your mother to answer for greater and lesser (deeds).
sentences.s491-s45: If he sends Sāv and Bāžam, it is good; no one’s skin will be torn in this battle.
sentences.s491-s46: Take heed of this clear command of mine, which my battle‑trumpeter/warrior will tell him.
sentences.s491-s47: You do that because of the kindness he showed you; as a reward, the nobleman's heart will be turned.
sentences.s491-s48: When you obtain this reply from the king, send him back on his way with kindness.
sentences.s491-s17: Take your seat in the rites and customs of the Kians; when I come before you, fasten your belt at your waist.
sentences.s491-s18: He ordered that the warlike fortress-warden take the neck-fetter (the chain) from his shoulder.
sentences.s491-s19: I will come before you as a petitioner; I will show you abundant humility.
sentences.s491-s20: He took a seat prepared apart from the council; if my entreaty grows, you will grant it to me.
sentences.s491-s13: Alexander realized who that man was and asked him what the remedy for that affair was.
sentences.s491-s14: He ordered that the vizier be brought before him and gave him a decree, a crown, and a throne.
sentences.s491-s15: The wise man was named Bitaghun; he was a certain counselor, a man of wide desires.
sentences.s491-s16: He said to him, 'The plan is: I will bring your bride before you, Alexander Philoquus.'
sentences.s491-s29: When that son of Qeydafeh — the city's conqueror — was brought, he was taken captive, weeping.
sentences.s491-s30: His wife likewise, with scent and color, had seized the young man, taking hold of him by the hand.
sentences.s491-s31: Sabk-e Bitqun said, 'Who is this man? From such pain, how many times must one weep?'
sentences.s491-s32: He answered thus: 'Bring back your wits — I am Pour Qeydafeh, Qeydrosh.'
sentences.s491-s9: A certain son of Qeydafeh was a son‑in‑law; in that city the royal farr smiled upon him and made him glad.
sentences.s491-s10: He had been given a noble daughter; his cap (i.e. his status) had been raised high at Qeydafeh.
sentences.s491-s11: They gave the son‑in‑law the bad name Qeyd‑rosh; the farr had given him heart, eye, and ear.
sentences.s491-s12: There was a certain ill‑famed man, a town‑besieger; by his hand a woman and her husband had been made captive.
sentences.s491-s33: Except for this radiant maiden, I have no match; she is kept hidden behind a veil from me.
sentences.s491-s34: I am resolved to take her to my own khan so that I may keep her as I would my own life.
sentences.s491-s35: I am now a captive in the besieger’s hand, my spirit weary from the stars and my body struck by arrows.
sentences.s491-s36: When Bitqun heard this speech from him, his head became full of pain and his heart full of blood.
